what_it_does: It presents a concrete example of a typed tree data model for a document-like substrate, showing node types, `part_of` parent-child edges, and a gesture algorithm that navigates the tree.
observations: YAML frontmatter records id, address, type `block`, source `claude_code`, state `current`, title `P4 · The typed tree (concrete)`, channel `dragnet-development`, and a `part_of` link to `board://item-e42d651f`.
The body draws an ASCII tree rooted at `doc:report` and descending through `section`, `heading`, `paragraph`, `sentence`, `word`, `list`, `list-item`, and `zone` nodes.
Each node is labeled with a `node_type` and connected via `part_of` edges.
It explicitly separates a `heading` node from the `section` node that owns everything below that heading.
It introduces `zone` nodes for the gaps between or after structural elements and says the parser must mint them.

It maps thumb gestures to pure tree-walking operations: tap selects the deepest node, thumb-up moves to the `part_of` parent, thumb-down moves to the first `part_of` child, left/right move between siblings, and release commits.
standouts: A heading node and its owned-content section are two distinct node types, making comment-on-heading-text different from comment-on-everything-below.
Structural gaps are first-class, addressable `zone` nodes rather than implicit whitespace.
The gesture algorithm has no document knowledge; it is only a cursor that walks `part_of` edges.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, links, and history fields.
Uses a Markdown code block for the ASCII tree and explanatory bullets afterward.
Names nodes with a `type:id` convention and a `node_type` attribute.
Relies on a single `part_of` relationship for the whole hierarchy.
concerns: The ownership rule `owns until next ≥-level heading` is stated informally and not formally defined.
The gesture semantics for overlapping or nested nodes at the same point are not specified.
There is no explicit node-type schema or list of allowed `node_type` values.
The concrete example is a single document shape; it does not show how the model handles tables, figures, or other structures.
notes: A design note that turns a document into a typed, edge-traversable tree and maps thumb gestures to pure tree-walking operations.
questions: What is the complete set of allowed `node_type` values?
How exactly are `zone` nodes bounded and parsed?
When tapping a point with multiple overlapping nodes, how is the deepest node resolved?
Does thumb-up stop at the immediate `part_of` parent or skip intermediate container nodes?
What does `release → commit` mean in the UI state machine?
What is the relationship between this item and `item-e42d651f`?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the title promises a concrete typed tree, and the body supplies a worked example plus traversal semantics.
apparent_themes: Separation of data model from interaction logic
Addressability of every structural element, including gaps
Tree-walking as a generic cursor mechanism
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a canonical, addressable typed-tree representation of documents so that selection and comment gestures can be implemented generically by walking edges rather than interpreting document semantics.
novelty: It treats structural gaps as first-class `zone` nodes and explicitly separates a heading node from the section node that owns everything below it, then reduces navigation to type-aware tree-edge walking.
contribution: A concrete node-type taxonomy and a gesture-to-tree-traversal mapping that keeps document semantics out of the interaction algorithm.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item that defines a concrete typed-tree document model with `part_of` edges, distinct heading and section nodes, addressable zone nodes, and a thumb-gesture cursor that walks the tree.
intention_for_embedding: To specify a canonical tree representation and generic traversal gestures for selecting and commenting on document structure.
